title: Understand: electromagnetic waves
description: Check your understanding of electromagnetic waves in this set of free, standards-aligned practice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
A model of the electromagnetic spectrum is shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Rank the following electromagnetic waves from highest frequency to lowest frequency.**

*highest frequency*

[[☃ sorter 1]]

*lowest frequency*
hints: All electromagnetic waves travel at the same speed of light in a vacuum. So, larger frequencies correspond to smaller wavelengths, and vice versa. As one increases the other decreases.
As we move from radio to gamma waves on the model, we see the wave peaks get closer together—wavelength *decreases*.
Frequency follows the opposite pattern and *increases* from radio to gamma waves.

So, the ranking from highest to lowest frequency is:

* X-rays
* visible light
* infrared
* radio

options.alt: Model of the EM spectrum. From longer wavelength to shorter wavelength, the bands are: radio, microwave, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, gamma.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select the true statements about electromagnetic waves.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Different parts of the electromagnetic spectrum have different frequencies and wavelengths.
Electromagnetic waves need a matter medium to travel through.
Human eyes can see all electromagnetic waves directly.
All electromagnetic waves travel at the speed of light in a vacuum.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select the true statements about electromagnetic waves.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Electromagnetic waves don't need a matter medium to travel through.
All parts of the electromagnetic spectrum have the same frequency and wavelength.
Human eyes cannot see all electromagnetic waves directly.
Some electromagnetic waves travel faster than others in a vacuum.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select the true statements about electromagnetic waves.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Different parts of the electromagnetic spectrum have different frequencies and wavelengths.
Electromagnetic waves don't need a matter medium to travel through.
Human eyes can see all electromagnetic waves directly.
Some electromagnetic waves travel faster than others in a vacuum.

assessment_items.question_content:
Two lights, a red laser and green laser, are shining through vacuum. Both lights have the same brightness.

**Which wave properties are different for the two lights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Visible light waves are part of the electromagnetic spectrum, as modeled below.

[[☃ image 1]]

As we move from red to green on the model, we see the wave peaks get closer together—wavelength decreases.

All electromagnetic waves travel at the same speed of light in a vacuum. The red and green light waves travel at the same speed. 

So, smaller wavelengths correspond to higher frequencies, and vice versa. As one increases the other decreases. Red light has a longer wavelength and lower frequency than green light.
Amplitude is related to the brightness of light. We're told that both lights have the same brightness. So, they have the same amplitude.
The wave properties that are different for the two lights are wavelength and frequency.

assessment_items.question_content:
Two lights, a red laser and green laser, are shining through vacuum. Both lights have the same brightness.

**Which wave properties are the same for the two lights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Visible light waves are part of the electromagnetic spectrum, as modeled below.

[[☃ image 1]]

As we move from red to green on the model, we see the wave peaks get closer together—wavelength decreases.

All electromagnetic waves travel at the same speed of light in a vacuum. The red and green light waves travel at the same speed. 

So, smaller wavelengths correspond to higher frequencies, and vice versa. As one increases the other decreases. Red light has a longer wavelength and lower frequency than green light.
Amplitude is related to the brightness of light. We're told that both lights have the same brightness. So, they have the same amplitude.
The wave properties that are the same for the two lights are speed and amplitude.

assessment_items.question_content:
Two red laser lights are shining through vacuum. The two lights have the same color. One is brighter than the other.

**Which wave properties are the same for the two lights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Visible light waves are part of the electromagnetic spectrum, as modeled below.

[[☃ image 1]]

A specific color of visible light has a specific wavelength. We're told both laser lights are the same red color. So, they have the same wavelength.

All electromagnetic waves travel at the same speed of light in a vacuum. Both lights travel at the same speed. 

And because both lights travel at the same speed, their equal wavelengths mean they must have equal frequencies too.
Amplitude is related to the brightness of light. We're told one light is brighter than the other. So, they must have different amplitudes.
The wave properties that are the same for the two lights are speed, frequency, and wavelength.

assessment_items.question_content:
When an electromagnetic wave reaches a material boundary, different results are possible.

**Identify each result described in the table.**

Result | Description
:- | :-
[[☃ dropdown 5]] | the wave crosses the boundary and travels through the material
[[☃ dropdown 4]] | the wave's energy is taken in by the material and converted to another form
[[☃ dropdown 3]] | the wave bounces back after reaching the boundary
hints:
When an electromagnetic wave reaches a material boundary, the wave may cross the boundary and travel through the material. This is called **transmission**. 

For example, visible light is transmitted through transparent materials like air, water, and glass.

It's also possible that the electromagnetic wave enters the material, but does not continue traveling through it. Instead, the wave's energy is converted to thermal energy or another energy form. This is called **absorption**.

For example, visible light is absorbed when it shines on a black object.

It's also possible that the electromagnetic wave doesn't pass through the boundary. Instead, the wave bounces back and continues traveling in its original material. This is called **reflection**.

For example, visible light is reflected from mirrors.

The answer is:

Result | Description
:- | :-
transmission | the wave crosses the boundary and travels through the material
absorption | the wave's energy is taken in by the material and converted to another form
reflection | the wave bounces back after reaching the boundary

assessment_items.question_content:
When an electromagnetic wave reaches a material boundary, different results are possible.

**Complete the descriptions.**

* If the wave bounces back after reaching the boundary, the wave is [[☃ dropdown 6]].

* If the wave's energy is taken in by the material and converted to another form, the wave is [[☃ dropdown 7]].

* If the wave crosses the boundary and travels through the material, the wave is [[☃ dropdown 8]].
hints:
When an electromagnetic wave reaches a material boundary, the wave may bounce back and continue traveling in its original material. This is called **reflection**.

For example, visible light is reflected from mirrors.

It's also possible that the electromagnetic wave enters the material, but does not continue traveling through it. Instead, the wave's energy is converted to thermal energy or another energy form. This is called **absorption**.

For example, visible light is absorbed when it shines on a black object.

It's also possible that the electromagnetic wave crosses the boundary and travels through the material. This is called **transmission**. 

For example, visible light is transmitted through transparent materials like air, water, and glass.

The answer is:

* If the wave bounces back after reaching the boundary, the wave is reflected.

* If the wave's energy is taken in by the material and converted to another form, the wave is absorbed.

* If the wave crosses the boundary and travels through the material, the wave is transmitted.

assessment_items.question_content:
When an electromagnetic wave reaches a material boundary, different results are possible.

**Match each result with its description by dragging the tiles.**

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints:
When an electromagnetic wave reaches a material boundary, it may enter the material but not continue traveling through it. Instead, the wave's energy is converted to thermal energy or another energy form. This is called **absorption**.

For example, visible light is absorbed when it shines on a black object.

It's also possible that the electromagnetic wave crosses the boundary and travels through the material. This is called **transmission**. 

For example, visible light is transmitted through transparent materials like air, water, and glass.

It's also possible that the electromagnetic wave doesn't pass through the boundary. Instead, the wave bounces back and continues traveling in its original material. This is called **reflection**.

For example, visible light is reflected from mirrors.

The answer is:

Result | Description
:- | :-
absorption | the wave's energy is taken in by the material and converted to another form
transmission | the wave crosses the boundary and travels through the material
reflection | the wave bounces back after reaching the boundary

right: the wave's energy is taken in by the material and converted to another form
the wave crosses the boundary and travels through the material
the wave bounces back after reaching the boundary
